ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for baby cabbage (娃娃菜); closest parent is Chinese cabbage food product (FOODON_03301710), which covers Brassica rapa subsp. pekinensis varieties including miniature forms.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers Chinese cabbage broadly; baby cabbage is a miniature cultivar of the same species and is mapped here as closest parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226723007 covers Chinese cabbage as a substance; baby cabbage is a miniature cultivar mapped here as closest parent.

main_ingredients: baby cabbage (娃娃菜, Brassica rapa subsp. pekinensis miniature cultivar)
oyster sauce or light soy sauce
allergens: soy (if soy sauce or oyster sauce used)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of tender green baby cabbage is approximately 250 g, presented as a whole dish of stir-fried or braised miniature cabbage heads with garlic and light seasoning.

nutrient_source.title: Chinese cabbage, raw (Brassica rapa subsp. pekinensis) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB No. 11119
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Baby cabbage (娃娃菜) is a miniature cultivar of Brassica rapa subsp. pekinensis (Chinese cabbage / napa cabbage). No dedicated baby cabbage entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for Chinese cabbage (raw) is used as the closest approved analogue. Nutrient values are for the raw vegetable; restaurant preparation (stir-frying with garlic and light sauce) will add modest sodium and fat. Values presented here are for the raw cabbage base per 100 g. Sodium is adjusted upward to reflect typical restaurant seasoning (oyster sauce / soy sauce) based on USDA Branded Food reference for stir-fried Chinese cabbage dishes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal); University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for baby cabbage (娃娃菜). GI is estimated at 15 based on the published GI of Chinese cabbage / napa cabbage (Brassica rapa subsp. pekinensis), which is classified as a non-starchy leafy vegetable with GI approximately 10–20 in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international tables. GL per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (15 × 7.5 g) / 100 = 1.1. Serving carbohydrate estimated as 3.0 g/100 g × 250 g serving / 100 = 7.5 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium in raw vegetable; moderate sodium added by restaurant seasoning (oyster sauce / soy sauce)
hypertension.guidance: Baby cabbage is inherently low in sodium (~9 mg/100 g raw), but restaurant preparation with oyster sauce or soy sauce can raise sodium to approximately 65–200 mg per 100 g; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sauce preparation and monitor portion size to limit sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~15) and low carbohydrate content (3 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Tender green baby cabbage has a very low glycemic index of approximately 15 and provides only 3 g of carbohydrate per 100 g, resulting in a negligible glycemic load of approximately 1.1 per restaurant serving, making it an excellent vegetable choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat, high folate, vitamin C, and potassium content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Baby cabbage is very low in saturated fat and cholesterol, and provides folate (66 mcg DFE/100 g), vitamin C (27 mg/100 g), and potassium (238 mg/100 g), nutrients associated with cardiovascular protection; it is a heart-healthy vegetable choice when prepared with minimal oil and sauce.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tender Green Baby Cabbage (嫩绿娃娃菜, wáwá cài) is a miniature cultivar of Chinese cabbage (Brassica rapa subsp. pekinensis), prized in Chinese cuisine for its tender, sweet leaves and compact, pale-green heads that resemble a small napa cabbage. Each head typically weighs 200–400 g and is harvested young, giving it a more delicate texture and milder flavour than full-sized napa cabbage. In Chinese restaurant cooking, baby cabbage is most commonly stir-fried with garlic and a light sauce of oyster sauce or light soy sauce, or braised in a superior stock, producing a silky, slightly sweet, and umami-rich dish. The vegetable is extremely low in calories (approximately 16 kcal per 100 g), very low in carbohydrates (3 g/100 g), and has a very low glycemic index of approximately 15, making it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or following a low-GI diet. It is a good source of folate (66 mcg DFE/100 g), vitamin C (27 mg/100 g), potassium (238 mg/100 g), and calcium (77 mg/100 g). The dish is naturally low in fat and saturated fat, supporting cardiovascular health. Sodium content is low in the raw vegetable but increases with restaurant seasoning; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sauce preparation. Baby cabbage is widely available in Chinese restaurants and zi char establishments across Singapore, often served as a shared vegetable side dish. It is suitable for vegetarians when prepared without oyster sauce, and is halal-friendly when prepared with appropriate condiments.

confidence_reason: Baby cabbage (娃娃菜) is a miniature cultivar of Brassica rapa subsp. pekinensis. No dedicated entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or USDA FoodData Central specifically for baby cabbage. Nutrient values are adapted from the USDA SR Legacy entry for Chinese cabbage (raw), which is the closest approved analogue. GI is estimated from published values for Chinese cabbage / non-starchy leafy vegetables in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021. Confidence is medium because the match is an adaptation rather than an exact entry.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB No. 11119 (Chinese cabbage, raw) as the closest approved analogue for baby cabbage (娃娃菜). No dedicated baby cabbage entry was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. 2. Sodium value of 65 mg/100 g reflects an estimate for the prepared dish (stir-fried with light sauce); raw Chinese cabbage contains approximately 9 mg/100 g sodium per USDA. 3. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate for non-starchy Chinese cabbage based on University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables; no direct GI measurement for baby cabbage was found. 4. saturated_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA source for raw Chinese cabbage (trace amounts below detection threshold). trans_fat_g is 0.0: naturally zero for a raw leafy vegetable. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: naturally zero as this is a plant food. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0: naturally zero as this is a plant food with no animal-derived components. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: naturally zero for a leafy vegetable not exposed to UV treatment. 5. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.0) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.1) are reported from USDA source. 6. All -1 sentinel values are not used in this record; all micronutrient fields are populated from USDA SR Legacy data. 7. The food_group has been corrected from 'Mixed' (input) to 'Vegetables' as baby cabbage is a leafy vegetable. 8. Multilingual names for Malay, Tamil, and Hindi are provided as this is a Chinese dish served in Singapore context, satisfying the Singaporean dish multilingual requirement. 9. Price data from field survey (SGD 21.84) is preserved in source metadata but not included in the RESULT template as price is not a template field.
